frontmatter.title: UXarray for Advanced HEALPix Analysis & Visualization

affiliations.name: NSF National Center for Atmospheric Research

children.label: UXarray for Advanced HEALPix Analysis & Visualization

children.value: package to perform advanced analysis operators over HEALPix data such as non-conservative zonal means, etc.
Working with HEALPix data - UXarray documentation
UXarray overview - Unstructured Grids Visualization Cookbook
Data visualization with UXarray - Unstructured Grids Visualization Cookbook

import cartopy.crs as ccrs
import intake
import uxarray as ux

children.value: We assume that you have already gone over the previous section,
UXarray for Basic HEALPix Statistics & Visualization
. If not and if you need to learn about data catalogs in general and the data we will use throughout this notebook, we recommend to check that section first.
Let us open the online catalog from the
WCRP’s Digital Earths Global Hackathon 2025 catalog repository
and read the output of the
, which is stored in the HEALPix format:

jupyter_data.name: stderr
jupyter_data.text:

children.value: Then let us pick a variable, the surface temperature, from the dataset, which will give us an

text/plain.content:
<xarray.UxDataArray 'ts' (time: 425, n_face: 12)> Size: 20kB
[5100 values with dtype=float32]
Coordinates:
  * time     (time) datetime64[ns] 3kB 2020-01-02 2020-01-03 ... 2021-03-01
Dimensions without coordinates: n_face
Attributes:
    grid_mapping:        crs
    hiopy::enable:       True
    hiopy::nnn:          4
    hiopy::time_method:  mean
    long_name:           surface temperature
    short_name:          
    standard_name:       surface_temperature
    units:               K

children.value: Computing the global surface temperature mean (at the first timestep) and also having a quick plot of it would be a good idea to have as references to compare the upcoming analyses & visualizations to them:

jupyter_data.name: stdout
jupyter_data.text: Global surface temperature average on  2020-01-02T00:00:00.000000000 :  286.9924  K

children.value:
%%time

projection = ccrs.Robinson()

uxda.isel(time=0).plot(
    projection=projection,
    cmap="inferno",
    features=["borders", "coastline"],
    title="Global surface temperature (Polygon raster)",
    width=700,
);

jupyter_data.name: stdout
jupyter_data.text:
CPU times: user 6.4 s, sys: 594 ms, total: 6.99 s
Wall time: 7.76 s

children.value: When working with a higher-resolution dataset at a global scale, it’s not always practical to render each cell as a polygon. Instead, we can rasterize the center of each pixel.

%%time
projection = ccrs.Robinson()

# Controls the size of each pixel (smaller value leads to larger pixels)
pixel_ratio = 0.5

uxda.isel(time=0).plot.points(
    projection=projection,
    rasterize=True,
    dynamic=False,
    width=1000,
    height=500,
    pixel_ratio=pixel_ratio,
    cmap="inferno",
    title=f"Global surface temperature (Point raster), pixel_ratio={pixel_ratio}",
);

jupyter_data.name: stdout
jupyter_data.text:
CPU times: user 278 ms, sys: 33.9 ms, total: 312 ms
Wall time: 452 ms

children.value: If we decrease the size of each pixel (by setting the pixel ratio to a higher value), we can start to see missing values, which is due to a lower density of points near the poles, leading to some pixels not containing any of our original points.
Because of this, it’s useful to try a few
values and see which one works best for your given resolution.

projection = ccrs.Robinson()

# Controls the size of each pixel (smaller value leads to larger pixels)
pixel_ratio = 2.0

uxda.isel(time=0).plot.points(
    projection=projection,
    rasterize=True,
    dynamic=False,
    width=1000,
    height=500,
    pixel_ratio=pixel_ratio,
    cmap="inferno",
    title=f"Global surface temperature (Point raster) with a bad pixel size selection, pixel_ratio={pixel_ratio}",
);
We can look at constant latitude/longitude cross-sections of an

boulder_lat = 40.0190


# With fine resolutions like zoom level of 9, it is visually hard to observe the cross-sections,
# so we will use a zoom level of 4 for a better visualization
uxda_coarse = ux.UxDataset.from_healpix(model_run(zoom=4, time="P1D").to_dask())["ts"]
uxda_coarse.uxgrid.face_node_connectivity

uxda_lat = uxda_coarse.cross_section.constant_latitude(boulder_lat)
uxda_lat

children.value:
import geoviews.feature as gf

uxda_lat.isel(time=0).plot(
    rasterize=False,
    projection=projection,
    global_extent=True,
    cmap="inferno",
    clim=(220, 310),
    features=["coastline"],
    title=f"Global surface temperature cross-section at {boulder_lat} degrees latitude",
    width=700,
) * gf.grid(projection=projection);
Let’s also look at the mean of the cross-section:

jupyter_data.text: Mean at 40.019 degrees lat (Boulder, CO, USA): 288.3074645996094 K

children.value:
import hvplot.xarray

(
    uxda.isel(time=0).plot(
        cmap="inferno",
        # periodic_elements="split",
        height=300,
        width=600,
        colorbar=False,
        ylim=(-90, 90),
    )
    + zonal_mean_ts.hvplot.line(
        y="latitudes",
        size=300,
        # width=180,
        # ylabel="",
        ylim=(-90, 90),
        xlim=(220, 310),
        # xticks=[220, 250, 280, 310],
        yticks=[-90, -45, 0, 45, 90],
    )
).opts(title="Temperature and its Zonal means at every 5 degree latitude")

children.value: Now, we will be looking into one of many possible use cases where remapping would be helpful.
The data set we have been using in this section so far belongs to the newer ICON simulation,
, while there is an older simulation as well,

, in the same catalog. They are both stored in the HEALPix format in this case, but for most of the model intercomparison workflows in general, they might not be even so. UXarray would still be helpful to remap of those model outputs to other and then make comparisons since it can support several most commonly used unstructrued grid formats.
In this particular case, we still have some use for UXarray’s remapping such that the newer simulation has the
as the maximum available resolution, while the older one has
though, there is no actual data simulated for
, the surface temperature. If there was, we could remap the newer simulation’s output to that one, so we could have both of them at
, and then we could look into the difference between them for instance. Let’s pretend the highest zoom-level in the newer data is
then, and remap that one into the older simulation’s grid.
Let’s start with opening the older simulation run first:

model_run_older = cat.online.icon_ngc4008

ds_older = model_run_older(zoom=8, time="P1D").to_dask()

uxds_older = ux.UxDataset.from_healpix(ds_older)
uxds_older

children.value: Plot the older simulation for reference

children.label: Plot the older simulation for reference

children.value: Let’s have a quick look at how the global surface temperature looks like in the older simulation’s output:

uxds_older["ts"].isel(time=0).plot(
    projection=projection,
    cmap="inferno",
    features=["borders", "coastline"],
    title="Global surface temperature (zoom = 8) - Older Simulation",
    width=700,
);
Visually there does not seem to be a huge difference between this and the newer simulation’s output we had plotted in the very beginning.
Remap the old simulation output to the newer one

children.label: Remap the old simulation output to the newer one

children.value: Let’s start remapping! For that, we will use the
of the newer simulation output as the destination grid and use an inverse distance weighted implementation:

%%time
uxda_older_remapped = uxds_older["ts"].isel(time=0).remap.inverse_distance_weighted(
    uxds.uxgrid, k=3, remap_to="face centers", coord_type="cartesian"
)

jupyter_data.name: stdout
jupyter_data.text:
CPU times: user 38.9 ms, sys: 3.15 ms, total: 42 ms
Wall time: 760 ms

/home/runner/micromamba/envs/healpix-cookbook-dev2/lib/python3.13/site-packages/uxarray/remap/utils.py:87: UserWarning: No spatial coordinate variables found in `source`.
  output_coords = coords_remapper.construct_output_coords()

children.value: Plot the difference between the old and newer simulations

children.label: Plot the difference between the old and newer simulations

children.value: Now that we have the older and newer model outputs on the same grid, let’s look at the surface temperature differences between the two:

(uxda.isel(time=0) - uxda_older_remapped).plot(
    projection=projection,
    cmap="RdBu_r",
    features=["borders", "coastline"],
    title="Global surface temperature difference between older and newer simulations (zoom = 9)",
    clim=(-25,25),
    width=700,
)

This ensures the aspect is respected, but means that the plot might be slightly larger than anticipated. Set the frame_width/frame_height explicitly to suppress this warning.
